speaker: interviewee
text: It's amazing-- amazing. [PAUSES FOR 22 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about the-- the crystal ball that broke during the earthquake. Go on [? with that. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the ball that I had mentioned that was saved during the night of the-- the Kristallnacht, the Kristallnacht. And I-- I had picked it up because it was standing on the floor, the-- outside of the cabinet that was being divided. And I brought it upstairs into the room to hide it behind the door. This crystal ball, of course, was very special to all of us. And when Diane saw the-- the broken crystal ball on the floor, nothing else mattered. She was heartbroken-- heartbroken. And at first, she didn't ever want to see any-- not even a tiny little part of it. So she picked it up, and she started to throw in-- pieces into the trash can. And then she stopped. She doesn't really know why. And she took a number of the pieces completely at random and put them in a bag and put them away. And that-- she kept it there for some two years, always in a corner in the closet. Every time she looked at it, she-- the sadness came back. But that's what-- you build up the house, you build up your-- your-- your unhappy feelings. And then one night, she had a dream. And she thought about the crystal ball and the broken pieces, and it came to her that it was like life. We have broken pieces in our life. Certainly, her grandparents and her mother had broken pieces and-- not only physically, not only in-- during the Crystal Night, but also in their lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she thought and she thought, and then she had a-- some kind of a revelation. She was taking painting lessons, and the husband or her paint-- of her-- her teacher was an artist, was a sculpturer. And she talked to him. And by that time, Mutti had died, and it was-- and she was only dead three years then. And it was still very close and very new and very painful to all of us. And she talked to this man and said, is there any chance that you could make a statue of my grandmother? And he said, well, I never met her. What shall-- how can we do that? She said, I have lots of tapes, videotapes. I have pictures, hundreds of pictures of her. And he said, let me have what you-- what-- what-- give me what you can. And he lived with those pictures and the video for I think three or four months.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] And he said, I think I can do it. And he poured-- he did the statue. And then he-- he-- I'm not familiar exactly what you do, and he made 14 copies, every one like this. One for my sister, one for me, one for all the grandchildren-- one for every grandchild. And then he said-- and Mutti, we always admired her hands. And she did-- talked a lot about her hands. And when she died, we took a white damask cloth, and we had all different colors out. And everybody put their hand into one of the colors. And we placed our hand on the damask cloth, and that went over the casket because we always-- Mutti was all-- and she-- when she blessed the candles, she always blessed them this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- and she-- she told him, you have to make-- to pay special attention to-- his-- her hands. And this is how it was. And then it came to her, why not take a piece of the ball-- the ball and put it on her lap under the hands. And there was room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is how everybody got the statue. And everybody has a different piece of glass-- I mean, the crystal. And that's how the crystal ball [? except-- ?] came to be, the crystal-- the memory of the crystal ball has remained. And it-- somebody heard about it, and they wrote to the And it was in every one of the editions all over the city-- north, south, east, and west.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing it with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's the story of the-- Mutti's ball.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents also go back to their villages-- [? religions ?] after?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father never would have-- or-- did or would have. My mother went once, when she visited me when-- when Shalom and I were in the-- in the ulpan in Israel, she came to visit us. And on the way back, she had planned to go to Krefeld just to go to the cemetery. Her father was buried there, and she wanted to visit him. And she was going to stay three days. And she went. She had made reservations. She was there for one day, couldn't stand it any more, and left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is your sister still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does she live?

speaker: interviewee
text: She now-- now lives in Laguna-- in Laguna Hills in Leisure World.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us that you went back visiting Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time when I was-- that was in 1968. I went-- we had an-- a-- I left in January. And the previous December, months before, my mother received a tape from a young group of-- a youth group in Krefeld who made it their-- their-- made it their business, their desire to find as many Jewish survivors as they could. And my mother's name came up and her address. And they sent her a tape for Christmas and-- and the new year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they wanted her to know that they were-- felt very sad about what happened to the Jews and that they would like to re-establish contact if possible and just wish them well and hope that they have happy holidays and so on. And then my mother called me. And they-- they found her address and sent it to her. And I said, what did you do with the tape? She said, I threw it in the trash can.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. That-- that night I couldn't sleep. And I kept saying to myself, these are 17-year-old children, exactly the age I was when I was-- when I left. They had nothing to do with it. At least we can be civil about it. I hadn't spoken German in 25 years. My German was very poor. I had no desire to speak it ever-- a word or two once in a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I called my mother in the morning. I said, you can't do that. You could at least tell them thank you for the tape or whatever you want, but you can't just ignore it. She said, if you want to, you do it. I don't want to do it. I said, Mutti, I can't speak enough German any more to let them know what I really want to say. So she said, I'll tell you what. Write it in English, and I will translate it for-- for you. I could still read German well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what we did. And I told the children over the-- told the young people that I appreciate-- that we appreciate the tape. I told them who I was and that-- that we-- Mutti would not talk to them, and I would like to-- I would-- I wanted them to know that I was exactly, I said, your age when I was-- had to leave, flee Germany. And-- and then I said, and I'm leaving in a month to the country of my people and I've-- I've always dreamed about all my life. I will be going to Israel. And possibly since I'm so close to Europe, I will visit some of my-- my relatives in Holland and Belgium. And we may even be going to my husband's family in Denmark-- never mentioned Germany. And then I said, and please give-- if any of my former girlfriends from class so and so, from the lyceum, read this or hear about this, please tell them I remember them. And I feel-- I hope they're all well and never said anything about re-establishing friendship. That was this-- oh, then-- and then we played Beethoven's Fifth. It was a short tape, and the rest of it was Beethoven's Fifth. And we sent it. And they published the whole thing word for word in the newspaper. And I was in Israel already. My husband, Eddie, was here. He couldn't go with us. And a big envelope came, a thick envelope. It was all the people who-- almost every girl I went to school with. You must come to Germany. And I said, I'm not going. I don't ever want to go. I had nightmares for years going over the German border, and I would wake up screaming at night. And I said, I can't do it. I'm not going back. And then finally, my husband convinced me that maybe you get over the nightmares, you will have to do it. So I consented under great protest. I didn't want to do it. And my girl-- one of the girls in my class who lives near Munich picked us up in [NON-ENGLISH]-- that's where we got off-- with her car. And she said-- she gave Eddie the keys, and she said-- she knew about my fear of the border. She said, Eddie, you drive, and Ruth and I will sit in the back. And we sat, and she talked and talked and talked. And I was turning my head. And all of a sudden, Eddie said, we're past the border, we're in Germany. So I never saw-- and I was very uncomfortable. But-- and I know that from Munich to-- went to Krefeld. And before I got into Krefeld, I was so sick. I threw up. I was-- I thought, I cannot enter that town. I just felt I couldn't do it. Memories-- my god, my grandparents' house, our house. All the memories. And then they-- I was-- I stayed with the-- also one of my school friends who is a doctor, physician. And they organized a class reunion. And that's how I got back. I went to the class reunion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like he walked into the room, and I said, you're so, you're, you're, you're. I knew everybody's name. And we fell into each other's arms. It was a-- very moving. They were just as scared and just as adamant about my being there, even though they wanted me to come. But they were so afraid of what it was going to be like. And I didn't want them to compare their-- they had a-- I knew they had a hard time. There was-- war is no fun for anyone. I knew that Krefeld was almost leveled. They-- they have a new mountain there with nothing but the debris of the city. And I was worried about them trying to compare their horror and their fear and their pain with what my people went through. I had a terrible time with that. But on the other hand, I couldn't ignore it. One lost a child, another one lost her sister in an air raid. I could not ignore it. One-- one of the girls never got to class because she was living in East Berlin and they wouldn't let her go. So-- and it worked out fine. I was to a certain point-- I listened. I didn't make any derogatory remarks. I didn't outright compare it, they did. They did that themselves. And it was a wonderful experience. And I-- we had at least four more since. Every time I come to Europe, even the time when we went on the reunion of the Jews of Krefeld, they made another class reunion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out about any of them, that they were Nazi collaborators, Nazi sympathizers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any of them? No. But we-- I know about one of my best friends then, whose husband was a-- was a pilot, did how-- did I don't know, 17 missions, I think. And he talks about-- he talked about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a strange feeling. Another one I walked into-- the one that all of us gives the-- it's always in her place-- has a menorah, a hanukkiah, standing in the corner. It drives me crazy every time I go there, I see that. And I never had the courage to ask her where she got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about concentration camps-- did they know anything about those? Did they talk about those?

speaker: interviewee
text: They ask what you-- we didn't outright talk-- they knew that we, Eddie and I, had gone to every-- to-- we-- we've been to seven different concentration camps. Why? Because I never did find out where my people really were killed. So I wanted to say Kaddish. I wanted to just go outside and scream. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we-- we went to-- to-- we were in Auschwitz. We were in-- in Bergen-Belsen. And we took-- and we even found one in Yugoslavia that nobody ever heard of called [? Nice. ?] And it was horror, horrible. So we've-- and we took all-- all our grandchildren except for Lauren and Shoshana have been with us. We took our son's four children, two-- two at a time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They have-- they were-- David was with them at least in three or four and we took-- two years ago, we took his two-- two youngest ones to Bergen-Belsen. And they know. I mean, when I get together with them, and I mention that I've taken my grandchild, that's enough. They know that-- that-- and then, of course, they say the horror. But they will also say, we've got to stop talking about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did they know? Do they say that they knew what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: During that time? Oh, no. Nobody knew. You know that. We went to Mauthausen, my husband and I. And we were going down the street where the concentration camp was. And as we were driving, I said to Eddie, stop the car, I want to do something. I got out of the car, and I rang the doorbell, and an old woman came out. And I said-- I said, I'm-- we're from America. And we're just interesting to know from you how long have you lived here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, 40, 50, [INAUDIBLE], she said for-- of what do I know? I said, what does-- was it like during the war years with the-- the concentration camp there? We knew from nothing. We had no idea what was going on there. I said, you're telling me that the-- the cars and the buses and the-- the-- the trucks that came by here, you didn't hear the screaming, and you didn't smell what was going on there? I did this four or five times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three of them listened to me, and they kept assuring me that they knew nothing about it. Two of them slammed the-- the door into my face. Nobody knew anything-- nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I just--

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't seen-- heard one single German that ever said they knew anything about it, what went on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's why I was asking whether any of your friends who wanted to see you and who you have-- you have relationship with, whether they ever mentioned that they knew--

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Afterwards. That--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, whether they told you after the war whether they knew while it was happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: They all said they didn't know. They all said they didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us how many grandchildren you have.

speaker: interviewee
text: 10. Now I have 11 because the oldest one is married. And our daughter has three. And our son, David, in New York has four, and Shalom has also three.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it you would like to leave behind for these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: What legacy?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: A close family. I would like them to remain the way they are now, being concerned with each other, learning from each other, loving each other, helping each other, remembering the past-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --knowing who they are and where they come from, what people they belong to. I want them to marry within the faith. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want them to know about their religion, either go to a school where they learn it or to a synagogue or-- or go to Israel, go on aliyah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I want them to replace-- help to replace what we have lost. I can't think of anything more important. Be honest, be-- and don't ever forget that no matter what other people-- what religion they are, what color they are, don't ever forget that they are human beings, that they must be respected. And don't ever be silent when you should speak up. That's what I want them to learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you for a wonderful testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: I appreciate this very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you permit us, we would like to show some of your pictures now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Happily. These are my three diaries, which I started when I was 12 years old, 1933, in Germany. And I stopped writing diary after Diane was born, 1944, and that was in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my diary-- this is my-- this is my passport. The J stands for Jew. And the little extra name Sara, in-- the-- right in front of Ruth Meyer, every German female had to add that to their name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish female.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Jewish woman. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish [? female. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here's the-- here's the inside of my German passport, and it was issued to me in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents on my father's side. My grandfather, Valentin Meyer, born in Krefeld, and my grandmother, Vilhelmina Wolff.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was taken in our backyard in-- in 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your backyard where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in my backyard in-- in the house on [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Krefeld. My grandparents on my mother's side. Amilia Meyer, her name was [NON-ENGLISH], and my grandfather, Marcus Meyer, in-- taken in Krefeld around the turn of the century.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The beginning of the-- the 20th century. These are my parents, Carl Meyer and Martha Meyer, before and after the wedding, in-- taken in Krefeld an-- on-- in 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name was also Meyer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right. Also Meyer. Our engagement picture. Eddie Elcott, Edward-- used to be Edward [? Elkowski-- ?] and Ruth Elcott. And my maiden name-- maiden name was Meyer. This was in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest of the family that you haven't met before is the family in New York. There's David standing on the right; his wife, Shira; the rabbi, who is sitting right next to him; and then in the second row-- in the first row way over on the left is Noam. He is their second child. And let's go down to the second row. On the-- on the left is Talia and Talia is 20, and Noam is 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we go to the 16-year-old Yaron, who is in center of the very first bottom row. And on his left is Liore, and she is 14. Now? On-- on the far left on the top row is 18-year-old Noam, David's son. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in December in Los Angeles. The-- the bride and the groom, Darin Karpel and Caryn Sacks.

speaker: interviewer
text: In December where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sinai Temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year in [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
